Commercial Apr 4, 2026

Delta Air Lines Cuts Flights Between Los Angeles & Anchorage Amid High Fuel Costs

Delta Air Lines decision to cancel its Los Angeles to Anchorage route reflects the broader challenges airlines face as fuel costs continue to rise.

Route cancellations due to fuel costs signal airlines are abandoning lower-demand markets rather than implementing industry-wide capacity reductions.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

United Airlines To Introduce Base Fare Option To Polaris & Premium Plus

The carrier has big plans for its premium cabins.

United's expansion of basic economy restrictions to premium cabins signals a shift toward unbundling ancillary services across all fare tiers, potentially reshaping cabin product definitions industry-wide.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

Why Premium Economy Isn’t Always Worth It

The poor value proposition behind many premium economy tickets, and why this is the case.

Premium economy's weak yield economics may force carriers to reconsider cabin configurations as narrow-body aircraft dominate routes where the cabin class struggles to justify its cost differential.

See Simple Flying →

Here’s How Much Faster The F-22 Is Compared To The F-35

The brutality of the first, and best, 5th-gen fighter.

The F-22's sustained supersonic speed advantage over the F-35 reflects a fundamental design trade-off between air superiority and multi-role versatility that continues shaping military aviation procurement decisions.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from investigating accidents post-facto to systematically identifying and mitigating hazards before they cause incidents.

Military Apr 4, 2026

Mission to Recover Downed F-15E Aircrew Puts Combat Search And Rescue in the Spotlight

The loss of an F-15E Strike Eagle in Iran prompted specialized Combat Search and Rescue units to spring into action, with a multitude of assets working together to recover all missing aircrew. Here’s how. On Apr. 3, 2026, multiple specialized units sprang into action for a complex mission they...

Modern CSAR operations require seamless coordination across multiple specialized units and assets, demonstrating the critical infrastructure needed to execute high-risk personnel recovery in contested airspace.

Space Force procurement expansion will likely drive aerospace contractor competition and create supply chain pressures affecting commercial aviation industry partnerships.

General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Eastern Iowa Airport's multi-use model balances flight training operations with commercial air carrier service, creating a hub where general aviation and scheduled carriers share infrastructure.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran’s RQ-170 Clone Destroyed in U.S. Strikes, CENTCOM Video Shows

A video shared by CENTCOM shows the destruction of an Iranian clone of the RQ-170 Sentinel, which was reverse-engineered after crashing in Iran 15 years ago. An Iranian clone of the secretive U.S. RQ-170 Sentinel ISR drone was apparently destroyed in an airstrike, an Apr. 2, 2026 video by the U.S....

Iran's reverse-engineered RQ-170 clone demonstrates how adversaries weaponize captured advanced ISR platforms, creating persistent air defense challenges for U.S. operations.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Illuminated in Orion

NASA astronaut Christina Koch reads on a tablet in the dimly lit Orion crew capsule in this April 3, 2026, photo. To the right of the image’s center, CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen is seen in profile peering out of one of Orion’s windows. Lights are turned off to avoid glare on...

Orion's cabin lighting design balances crew comfort with window visibility requirements, a critical factor for deep-space mission success and astronaut operational efficiency.

See NASA →

The aircraft offers exceptional long-haul capabilities.

The 787's cockpit design and fuel efficiency advantages have created pilot preference that influences airline fleet acquisition decisions and training investments.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

Reno-Tahoe's Mustang 9 Departure requires precise adherence to specific procedural steps from both parallel runways to avoid terrain and airspace conflicts in the mountainous Nevada region.

Why The Airbus A220 Is Becoming More Attractive To Southwest Airlines

A closer look at what the aircraft could mean for Southwest, and why its 737-only operations are now at risk.

Southwest's iconic 737-only strategy is eroding as the A220's superior fuel efficiency and lower operating costs become economically compelling for their high-frequency, point-to-point network.

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Artemis 2 in good shape cruising towards the moon

A day after lighting its engine to head to the moon, the Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft is performing well with only minor issues.

Artemis 2's successful trans-lunar injection burn demonstrates NASA's readiness for crewed lunar missions, validating the Space Launch System's reliability after years of development delays.

Industry Apr 4, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-airspace systems can simultaneously address capacity constraints and environmental impact as air traffic continues accelerating.

See MDPI →

Regulatory Apr 4, 2026

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

Eliminating EAS funding would force 44 rural communities to lose their only commercial air access, potentially reshaping regional aviation networks and airline route planning.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's larger Cygnus XL variant increases ISS cargo capacity to 11,000 pounds, expanding commercial resupply capabilities for orbital operations.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US A-10 hit during Iran rescue mission, pilot ejects and is rescued

A US Air Force A-10 was hit during combat operations tied to the war with Iran on April…...

Combat damage to A-10s during Middle Eastern operations highlights the aircraft's vulnerability despite its robust design, raising questions about force protection in extended regional conflicts.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's aviation credentials as a certified transport pilot bring operational expertise to NTSB leadership that strengthens accident investigation credibility with the pilot community.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

US and Europe revise GPS interference guidelines as spoofing risks grow

Aviation authorities are warning that instances of GPS interference have spread across a widening range of high-risk airspace,…...

Escalating GPS spoofing incidents are forcing regulators to strengthen real-time detection and backup navigation protocols across critical airspace zones.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Updated: A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashed Near the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Officials Tell NYT

Almost the same time as an F-15E Strike Eagle went down over Iran, the U.S. Air Force lost an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft near the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. officials told NYT that the jet’s pilot was safely rescued. While search and rescue efforts for at least one of the crew members of an […]...

Two separate U.S. Air Force combat losses in the same timeframe over the Persian Gulf region signal heightened operational tempo and potential adversary air defense activity in a critical strategic corridor.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Meet NASA’s New Artemis II Science Officers

NASA’s first Artemis II science officers—Kelsey Young, Trevor Graff, and Angela Garcia—are pioneering a new era of mission operations by integrating advanced lunar science directly into Mission Control. Their work will shape how future Artemis crews observe, analyze, and deepen our understanding of...

NASA's new science officer roles embed real-time lunar research expertise in Mission Control, fundamentally restructuring how crewed space missions integrate scientific discovery into operational decision-making.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Italy’s Argotec plans to scale Florida satellite facility to meet rising US demand

Italy’s Argotec has officially opened its first U.S. satellite production facility, cementing a foothold near Kennedy Space Center in Florida to join other foreign space firms pursuing growing demand from American programs.

European satellite manufacturers are establishing domestic U.S. production to secure contracts in America's expanding space economy and reduce supply chain vulnerabilities.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US F-15E Shot Down Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued

The fate of the aircraft’s second crew member is unknown.

The shootdown highlights advanced Iranian air defense capabilities and raises operational concerns for US tactical aviation in contested Middle Eastern airspace.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA report proposes sweeping rewrite of Part 141 flight training rules

A 471-page industry report released through the FAA’s Part 141 modernization docket proposes a sweeping rewrite of how…...

The FAA's 471-page modernization proposal signals structural overhaul of flight training standards, likely reshaping pilot certification timelines and curriculum requirements across the industry.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom

Today marks the 100th anniversary of the birth of Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom, born April 3, 1926, in Mitchell, Indiana. As one of NASA’s first seven astronauts, he became America’s second astronaut to fly in space when he launched aboard the Liberty Bell 7 spacecraft on July 21, 1961, just weeks after...

Grissom's pioneering Mercury missions established critical early spaceflight protocols that shaped subsequent NASA operations and astronaut selection standards.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 3, 2026

A C-130J awaits its next assignment on the flightline.

The C-130J's enduring presence on active flightlines underscores the military transport aircraft's continued operational relevance decades after its introduction.

Electric Apr 3, 2026

ZeroAvia edges closer to cert as FAA issues electric engine conditions

ZeroAvia has this week celebrated a “major step” towards certification of its ZA600 hydrogen-electric powertrain, after the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) published special conditions to certify the system’s electric engine component. The FAA published 33 special conditions for...

FAA's 33 special conditions establish the regulatory framework for hydrogen-electric powertrains, signaling imminent certification timelines for ZeroAvia's regional aircraft propulsion system.

See Runway Girl Network →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

White House again proposes steep NASA budget cuts

For the second consecutive year, the White House is proposing a major budget cut for NASA that would significantly impact the agency’s science programs and the International Space Station.

Sustained NASA budget cuts threaten ISS operations and long-term aerospace industry contracts, disrupting commercial space partnerships and launch vehicle development pipelines.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Test Pilots and the Road Back to the Moon

For NASA’s first crewed lunar mission since Apollo, aviation experience is one of the clearest threads linking the astronauts aboard Orion.

NASA's Artemis astronauts' aviation backgrounds underscore how test pilot expertise remains critical for lunar exploration despite six decades of technological advancement.
